sections.heading: Taking investment instrument or intermediated security free
sections.title: Taking investment instruments or intermediated securities free
sections.summary: This section specifies conditions under which investment instruments or intermediated securities can be taken free of security interests. It outlines the circumstances where such assets are no longer subject to a security interest, typically when the security interest is no longer enforceable or has been discharged.

requirements.text: Investment instruments or intermediated securities can be taken free of security interests under specified conditions

sections.source_quote: Taking investment instrument or intermediated security free

sections.heading: Execution creditor has priority over unperfected security
sections.title: Execution creditor priority over unperfected security
sections.summary: This section establishes that an execution creditor has priority over unperfected security interests when enforcing a security interest. It ensures that creditors who have executed on collateral can take priority over less perfected security interests in the event of default.

requirements.text: Execution creditor has priority over unperfected security interests when enforcing a security interest

sections.source_quote: Execution creditor has priority over unperfected security

sections.heading: Rights on transfer of account or chattel paper—contractual
sections.title: Contractual rights on transfer of account or chattel paper
sections.summary: This section details contractual rights that arise upon the transfer of accounts or chattel paper. It specifies the conditions under which parties can retain rights to the transferred property, typically through contractual agreements rather than legal security interests.

requirements.text: Contractual rights on transfer of account or chattel paper are established under specified conditions

sections.source_quote: Rights on transfer of account or chattel paper—contractual

sections.heading: Priority of purchase money security interest in processed or
sections.title: Priority of purchase money security interest in processed property
sections.summary: This section addresses the priority of purchase money security interests in processed or commingled personal property. It specifies that such security interests take priority over other unperfected interests in the event of a dispute over the property.

requirements.text: Purchase money security interest in processed or commingled property takes priority over other unperfected interests

sections.source_quote: Priority of purchase money security interest in processed or

sections.heading: Section 1
sections.title: Personal Property Securities Act 2009 compilation section
sections.summary: This section provides a compilation of the Personal Property Securities Act 2009, including its general application, the definition of personal property, security interests, and the conditions for perfection and priority of security interests. It outlines the scope of the Act and its applicability to various types of property and interests.

requirements.text: Personal property includes tangible and intangible property other than real property
A security interest is enforceable against a grantor when it attaches to collateral

A security interest is enforceable against third parties when it has attached to collateral and either the secured party has possession or control of the collateral, or a security agreement covers the collateral

A security interest is perfected if it has attached to collateral, is enforceable against third parties, and certain extra steps (possession or control of the collateral, or registration on the Register of Personal Property Securities) have been taken
The Register of Personal Property Securities enables secured parties to give notice of actual or prospective security interests

sections.source_quote: Section 1: Compilation An Act relating to personal property securities, and for related purposes

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions of financial and property terms in the Australian Securities and Investments Commission Act
sections.summary: This section defines key terms related to financial and property matters, including accounts, ADI accounts, advances, and security interests. It specifies that a security interest secures payment or performance of an obligation and may arise from certain transactions like transfers of accounts. The definitions cover terms such as account debtor, Australian entity, and business day, with examples provided for clarity.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish foundational terms for financial and property transactions that could impact data transfers, particularly regarding security interests and accounts. They help clarify what constitutes personal property and financial instruments relevant to cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: A security interest is an interest in personal property provided for by a transaction that secures payment or the performance of an obligation

Certain transactions that do not secure payment or the performance of an obligation may also give rise to a security interest: transfers of accounts, consignments and certain long-term leases and bailments (called PPS leases)

An account means a monetary obligation arising from disposing of property, granting a right, providing services, an ADI account, chattel paper, an intermediated security, an investment instrument, or a negotiable instrument
An account debtor means a person who is obligated under an account or chattel paper
ADI (short for authorised deposit-taking institution) has the same meaning as in the Banking Act 1959
ADI account means an account kept by a person with an ADI that is payable on demand or at some time in the future
Advance means the payment of currency, the provision of credit or the giving of value, including any liability of a debtor to pay interest, credit costs and other charges
After-acquired property means personal property acquired by the grantor after a security agreement is made
Australian entity means any individual located in Australia, a company registered under the Corporations Act 2001, a corporation sole, or a public authority
Business day means a day other than Saturdays, Sundays, public holidays, Christmas Day to New Year's Day, or days when the Registrar has refused access to the register

Chattel paper means one or more writings that evidence a monetary obligation and either or both of the following: a security interest in specific goods, specific intellectual property, a document of title, an intermediated security, an investment instrument, or a negotiable instrument
Commercial consignment means a consignment if the consignor retains an interest in goods delivered to the consignee for sale, lease or other disposal in the ordinary course of business
Commercial property means personal property other than consumer property
Company means a company registered under Part 2A.2 or Part 5B.1 of the Corporations Act 2: a registrable body registered under Division 1 or 2 of Part 5B.2 of that Act
Crops means crops (whether matured or not and whether naturally grown or planted) that have not been harvested
Currency means currency authorised as a medium of exchange by the law of Australia or of any other country
Debtor means a person who owes payment or performance of an obligation secured by a security interest in personal property, or a transferee of, or successor to, an obligation
Defect, in relation to a registration, includes an irregularity, omission or error in the registration
Description of personal property (including collateral and proceeds) means a description that identifies the item or class of personal property
Document of title means a writing issued by or addressed to a bailee that covers goods in the bailee's possession that are identified or fungible portions of an identified mass

sections.source_quote: Section 10: A security interest is an interest in personal property provided for by a transaction that secures payment or the performance of an obligation. The form of the transaction and the identity of the person who has title to the property do not affect whether an interest is a security interest. Certain transactions that do not secure payment or the performance of an obligation may also give rise to a security interest: transfers of accounts, consignments and certain long-term leases and bailments (called PPS leases).

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions of security interests and personal property terms
sections.summary: This section defines key terms related to security interests and personal property under Australian law. It specifies that a security interest secures payment or performance of an obligation without regard to transaction form. It lists examples including fixed charges, floating charges, chattel mortgages, and conditional sales. The section also clarifies that security interests exclude licenses and certain regulated interests, and specifies conditions under which security interests are created.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines critical terms for data transfers involving security interests, but does not directly impact cross-border data transfers or DTIA requirements as it focuses on financial security interests rather than personal data.

requirements.text: A security interest means an interest in personal property provided for by a transaction that secures payment or performance of an obligation

A security interest includes fixed charges, floating charges, chattel mortgages, conditional sales, hire purchase agreements, pledges, trust receipts, consignments, leases of goods, assignments, transfers of title, and flawed asset arrangements
A security interest also includes interests of transferees under transfers of accounts or chattel paper
A security interest includes the interest of consignors under commercial consignments
A security interest includes the interest of lessors or bailors under PPS leases
A person who owes payment to another may take a security interest in the other person's right to payment
An account debtor may take a security interest in an account or chattel paper
An ADI may take a security interest in an ADI account
A security interest does not include licenses
A security interest does not include interests prescribed by regulations
A security interest is not created only by agreements to postpone or subordinate rights

sections.source_quote: Section 12: A security interest means an interest in personal property provided for by a transaction that, in substance, secures payment or performance of an obligation (without regard to the form of the transaction or the identity of the person who has title to the property).

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Definition of intermediary and securities account
sections.summary: This section defines what constitutes an intermediary and a securities account. An intermediary is only active in that role when acting as such, and a person is not considered an intermediary merely because they maintain a securities account for an issuer. A securities account is defined as an account where interests in financial products can be credited or debited, or for intermediaries, a record of holdings and transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines key terms for security interests, critical for understanding who qualifies as an intermediary and how securities accounts function in cross-border data contexts.

requirements.text: An intermediary is only active in that role while acting as an intermediary
A person is not an intermediary for the purposes of paragraph (2)(a) or (c) merely because they maintain a securities account on behalf of an issuer
A person is not an intermediary for the purposes of paragraph (2)(a) or (c) merely because they act as a central securities depository, registrar or transfer agent
A person is not an intermediary for the purposes of paragraph (2)(a) or (c) merely because they record details of interests in financial products in their own books
A securities account means an account to which interests in financial products may be credited or debited
For intermediaries, a securities account means a record of holdings and transfers of interests in financial products

sections.source_quote: Section 15(4) to (7) of the Act: An intermediary (including a central securities depository) is an intermediary only while acting in the capacity of an intermediary. A person is not an intermediary for the purposes of paragraph (2)(a) or (c) merely because the person maintains a securities account on behalf of the issuer of the financial products to which the account relates. A securities account means: (a) an account to which interests in financial products may be credited or debited; or (b) in the case of an intermediary mentioned in paragraph 15(2)(b)—a record of holdings and transfers of interests in financial products.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security interest perfection rules for collateral
sections.summary: This section outlines how security interests are perfected for different types of collateral. Perfection occurs through registration, possession, or control of collateral. It specifies that a security interest is perfected if it is temporarily perfected by registration, possession, or control, and that certain types of collateral like ADI accounts, intermediated securities, and negotiable instruments have specific perfection rules.
sections.dtia_summary: Details how security interests are perfected across different collateral types, critical for understanding data transfer safeguards when assets are held by intermediaries.

requirements.text: A security interest is perfected if it is temporarily perfected or otherwise perfected by force of the Act
A security interest is perfected if the security interest is attached to collateral and enforceable against third parties
A security interest is perfected if registration is effective with respect to the collateral
A security interest is perfected if the secured party has possession of the collateral (other than possession as a result of seizure or repossession)

A security interest is perfected if the secured party has control of the collateral, including ADI accounts, intermediated securities, investment instruments, negotiable instruments not evidenced by a certificate, and letters of credit
A security interest may be perfected regardless of the order in which attachment and perfection steps occur
A single registration may perfect one or more security interests

sections.source_quote: Section 21(1) to (4) of the Act: A security interest in particular collateral is perfected if: (a) the security interest is temporarily perfected, or otherwise perfected, by force of this Act; or (b) all of the following apply: (i) the security interest is attached to the collateral; (ii) the security interest is enforceable against a third party; (iii) subsection (2) applies. This subsection applies if: (a) for any collateral, a registration is effective with respect to the collateral; or (b) for any collateral, the secured party has possession of the collateral (other than possession as a result of seizure or repossession); or (c) for the following kinds of collateral, the secured party has control of the collateral: (i) an ADI account; (ii) an intermediated security; (iii) an investment instrument; (iv) a negotiable instrument that is not evidenced by a certificate; (v) a right evidenced by a letter of credit that states that the letter of credit must be presented on claiming payment or requiring the performance of an obligation; (vi) satellites and other space objects.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Control of investment instruments and negotiable instruments
sections.summary: This section defines when a person has control over investment instruments, including those evidenced by certificates or not. Control is established through registration as the registered owner, possession of the instrument, or an agreement allowing the person to initiate transfers or deal with the instrument. The rules specify conditions for control in different scenarios, including electronic messaging and agreements with issuers.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines control mechanisms for investment instruments, critical for determining data transfer authority across borders where instruments are involved in financial transactions.

requirements.text: A person has control of an investment instrument if the issuer registers the person as the registered owner
A person has control of an investment instrument evidenced by a certificate if they possess it and can transfer or deal with it
A person has control of an investment instrument not evidenced by a certificate if there is an agreement allowing them to initiate transfers
A secured party does not have control of a letter of credit unless the issuer consents to assigning proceeds
A secured party has control of a negotiable instrument not evidenced by a certificate if it can be transferred via clearing facility rules and there is an agreement controlling electronic messages

sections.source_quote: Section 27(2): A person, other than the debtor or grantor, has control of an investment instrument if the issuer of the instrument registers the person as the registered owner of the instrument.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Temporary perfection of security interests in collateral
sections.summary: This section outlines temporary perfection rules for security interests in collateral when goods or instruments are returned to the grantor or debtor. It specifies that security interests become temporarily perfected for 5 business days after the action time (e.g., sale, exchange) and revert to unperfected status unless perfected otherwise within that period. The rules apply to both physical goods and negotiable instruments.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical for DTIAs as it defines temporary perfection periods for security interests in cross-border data transfers involving collateral, ensuring timely reattachment and compliance with temporary perfection rules.

requirements.text: A security interest in goods temporarily perfected for 5 business days after action time (e.g., sale, exchange)
Security interest becomes unperfected after 5 business days unless perfected otherwise
Security interest reattaches to goods if they come into possession of grantor or debtor after sale or lease

sections.source_quote: Section 35(2): This subsection covers the period starting at the action time and ending at the end of 5 business days after the day the action time occurs.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Deemed goods security interest and temporary perfection periods
sections.summary: This section establishes that when goods are sold or leased and the account or chattel paper is transferred, the transferee acquires a deemed security interest in the goods if they return to the transferor or transferee under specific circumstances. The security interest attaches at repossession time and is temporarily perfected for 5 business days from that time, after which it becomes unperfected unless otherwise perfected.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines temporary perfection periods for security interests in goods when relocated across borders, impacting how data transfers involving collateral must be assessed for temporary security interest validity.

requirements.text: If a sale or lease creates an account or chattel paper and it is transferred, the transferee has a deemed security interest in the goods if they return to transferor or transferee under specified circumstances
The deemed security interest attaches to goods at repossession time
The deemed security interest is temporarily perfected for 5 business days from repossession time
The deemed security interest becomes unperfected after 5 business days unless otherwise perfected

sections.source_quote: If a sale or lease of goods creates an account or chattel paper, and the account or chattel paper is transferred to another person, the transferee is taken to have a security interest in the goods if, at a particular time (the repossession time) the goods come into the possession of the transferor, or of the transferee, in any of the following circumstances

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Taking personal property free of security interests
sections.summary: This section outlines rules for when personal property may be acquired free of security interests, including unperfected interests, serial number defects, motor vehicles, and transactions in the ordinary course of business. It specifies conditions under which buyers or lessees take property free of security interests without subrogation rights.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs when personal property can be transferred without security interests, relevant for data transfers involving physical assets across borders.

requirements.text: A buyer or lessee takes personal property free of unperfected security interest if not part of a transaction they created
A buyer or lessee takes personal property free of security interest if serial number is correct and not registered
A buyer or lessee takes motor vehicles free of security interest if serial number is correct and seller is authorized
A buyer or lessee takes personal property free of security interest in ordinary course of business
A buyer or lessee takes personal property free of security interest for personal use if market value is $5,000 or less
A holder takes currency free of security interest if no knowledge of security interest

sections.source_quote: A buyer or lessee of personal property, for value, takes the personal property free of an unperfected security interest in the property.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security interest priority in financial collateral
sections.summary: This section establishes priority rules for security interests in financial collateral. It specifies that perfected security interests take priority over unperfected ones, and that perfected interests are prioritized based on the order of perfection. The rules also address how security interests in proceeds of collateral are treated relative to the original collateral, and include exceptions for purchase money security interests and control-based perfection.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by defining priority rules for security interests across borders, particularly for financial collateral and proceeds. It requires careful assessment of security interest perfection methods and timing to ensure compliance with cross-border data transfer requirements.

requirements.text: A perfected security interest in collateral has priority over an unperfected security interest in the same collateral
Priority between unperfected security interests in the same collateral is determined by the order of attachment
A security interest perfected by control has priority over a security interest perfected by other means
A security interest in proceeds of original collateral has the same priority as the security interest in the original collateral
A purchase money security interest in inventory has priority over other perfected security interests in the same inventory
A purchase money security interest in personal property has priority over other perfected security interests if perfected by registration within 15 business days
A non-purchase money security interest in an account has priority over a purchase money security interest in the account as proceeds of inventory
A secured party may subordinate their security interest to another interest in the collateral

sections.source_quote: Section 55(3): A perfected security interest in collateral has priority over an unperfected security interest in the same collateral.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Priority of security interests in transferred collateral
sections.summary: This section establishes priority rules between security interests (transferor-granted and transferee-granted) when collateral is transferred. It specifies conditions under which the transferor-granted interest has priority, including continuous perfection since transfer, and when the transferee-granted interest takes priority after re-perfection and notification to other secured parties. The rules also address exceptions for specific scenarios like negotiable instruments and chattel paper.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines priority rules for security interests in transferred collateral, which could impact cross-border data transfers if data is treated as collateral. It specifies conditions for priority between security interests that may affect data transfer arrangements.

requirements.text: The transferor-granted interest has priority if it was perfected immediately before transfer and has been continuously perfected since transfer.

The transferor-granted interest has priority if it was perfected by registration immediately before transfer, becomes unperfected, is later re-perfected, and a notice is given to all other secured parties who have a registration describing the collateral.

The transferee-granted interest has priority if the transferor-granted interest is re-perfected, the transferee acquires collateral without actual or constructive knowledge of breach, and the transferee-granted interest secures performance of an advance made before re-perfection.
The interest of a creditor who receives payment of a debt through electronic funds transfer has priority over security interests in funds paid and intangible sources.
A person who acquires a negotiable instrument without actual or constructive knowledge of breach has priority over a perfected security interest in the instrument.
A person who acquires chattel paper in the ordinary course of business for new value has priority over a perfected security interest in the chattel paper.
The holder of a negotiable document of title has priority over a perfected security interest if they acquired it without actual or constructive knowledge of breach.
An execution creditor has priority over any unperfected security interest in collateral at the time of seizure.
A perfected security interest held by an ADI in an ADI account has priority over other perfected security interests in the account.

sections.source_quote: Section 66: The transferor-granted interest has priority if it was perfected immediately before the transfer and has been continuously perfected since the transfer.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security interests in agricultural products and livestock
sections.summary: This section establishes rules for security interests in agricultural products and livestock. It specifies that security interests can attach to crops while growing and to livestock products before they become proceeds. It also outlines priority rules for security interests in crops and livestock, requiring that priority interests be granted for value and to enable production or development of the assets.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by defining conditions under which security interests in agricultural products and livestock can be established without prejudice to land rights or other interests, affecting cross-border transfers of such assets.

requirements.text: A security interest may attach to crops while they are growing
A security interest may attach to the products of livestock before they become proceeds
A perfected security interest in crops has priority over other interests if granted for value and to enable production
A perfected security interest in livestock has priority over other interests if granted for value and to enable development
The priority interest must be granted within 6 months of the security agreement for crops or livestock

sections.source_quote: A security interest may attach to crops while they are growing. A security interest may attach to the products of livestock before they become proceeds (for example, the wool on a sheep's back before the sheep is shorn).

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security interests in processed or commingled goods
sections.summary: This section addresses security interests in goods that become part of a product or mass through processing or commingling. It states that security interests continue in the product or mass if the identity of the original goods is lost, and perfection of the security interest applies to the product or mass for default priority rules.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by specifying that security interests in processed or commingled goods continue in the resulting product, which may impact cross-border data transfers involving such goods.

requirements.text: A security interest in goods that become part of a product or mass continues in the product or mass if identity is lost
The identity of goods is lost in a product or mass if it is not commercially practical to restore the goods to their original state
Perfection of a security interest in processed or commingled goods applies to the product or mass for default priority rules

sections.source_quote: A security interest in goods that subsequently become part of a product or mass continues in the product or mass if the goods are so manufactured, processed, assembled or commingled that their identity is lost in the product or mass.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Priority rules for security interests in processed or commingled goods
sections.summary: This section establishes priority rules for security interests in processed or commingled goods. It limits the priority of any security interest to the value of the goods on the day they became part of the product or mass. It also specifies that perfected security interests have priority over unperfected ones, and outlines how multiple perfected or unperfected interests share the product or mass proportionally based on the obligations they secure. The obligation secured by a security interest does not exceed the value of the goods on the day they became part of the product or mass.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer impact assessments by establishing priority rules for security interests in processed or commingled goods, which could impact how data is shared or transferred across borders when goods are processed or commingled.

requirements.text: Any priority that a security interest continuing in the product or mass has over another security interest in the product or mass is limited to the value of the goods on the day they became part of the product or mass.
A perfected security interest continuing in a product or mass has priority over an unperfected security interest continuing in the same product or mass.

If more than one perfected security interest continues in the same product or mass, each perfected security interest is entitled to share in the product or mass according to the ratio that the obligation secured by the perfected security interest bears to the sum of the obligations secured by all perfected security interests in the same product or mass.

If more than one unperfected security interest continues in the same product or mass, each unperfected security interest is entitled to share in the product or mass according to the ratio that the obligation secured by the unperfected security interest bears to the sum of the obligations secured by all unperfected security interests in the same product or mass.
The obligation secured by a security interest does not exceed the value of the goods on the day the goods became part of the product or mass.

sections.source_quote: Any priority that a security interest continuing in the product or mass has over another security interest in the product or mass is limited to the value of the goods on the day on which they became part of the product or mass.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Rights and remedies for security interests in collateral
sections.summary: This section outlines the rights and remedies available to secured parties when enforcing security interests in collateral. It specifies that secured parties may deal with collateral only to the extent the grantor would be entitled to do so, with exceptions for when the secured party had title to the collateral or when a transfer by the grantor would be prohibited. It also states that secured parties may only seize, purchase, or dispose of licenses subject to the terms of the license and applicable laws.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it governs the enforcement of security interests in collateral, which could impact how data is handled or transferred across borders when security interests are involved.

requirements.text: In exercising rights and remedies provided by this Chapter, a secured party may deal with collateral only to the same extent as the grantor would be entitled to so deal with the collateral.
Subsection (1) does not apply if the secured party had title to the collateral immediately before starting to exercise any right or remedy provided by this Part.

Subsection (1) does not apply to the extent that it would otherwise prevent the secured party from dealing with the collateral by way of transfer because a transfer by the grantor would be prohibited or declared to be a default under a security agreement.
A secured party may only seize, purchase or dispose of a licence subject to the terms and conditions of the licence and any applicable law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory.

sections.source_quote: In exercising rights and remedies provided by this Chapter, a secured party may deal with collateral only to the same extent as the grantor would be entitled to so deal with the collateral.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Enforcement of security interests in personal property
sections.summary: This section outlines how a secured party may enforce a security interest in personal property. It permits enforcement methods similar to land law, allows for the application of land law principles with modifications, and specifies that the decision of the first secured party does not limit the rights of other secured parties. The section also includes provisions for higher priority parties to have standing in proceedings and the ability to apply for judicially supervised sales.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by requiring that security interests in personal property be enforced with similar methods to land law, allowing for the application of land law principles with modifications, and ensuring that higher priority parties retain rights to enforce their security interests.

requirements.text: A notice must contain a description of the personal property to which the notice relates
A notice must set out the effect of this section
The secured party may enforce the security interest in the same way as the interest in land may be enforced under land law
Law in the same terms as land law applies for enforcement of security interests in personal property
The decision of the secured party under paragraph 117(2)(b) does not limit the rights of other secured parties
The other secured party has standing in proceedings taken by the first secured party
The other secured party may apply to a court for judicially supervised sale
The court may grant the application for judicially supervised sale
The regulations may modify the law that applies by virtue of subsection (4)

sections.source_quote: The secured party may enforce the security interest in the same way, with any necessary modification, as the interest in the land may be enforced under the land law.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Secured party's duty to obtain market value before disposing collateral
sections.summary: A secured party disposing of collateral under section 128 (excluding purchases) must exercise reasonable care to obtain at least the market value of the collateral before disposal. This duty applies to the secured party and the grantor, and requires the secured party to consider the market value at the time of disposal. The duty does not apply to disposals by purchase as specified in the note.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a duty for secured parties to maximize collateral value before disposal, which impacts data transfer impact assessments when collateral includes personal data. The obligation to obtain market value affects how data is valued and transferred across borders.

requirements.text: A secured party who disposes of collateral under section 128 (other than by purchasing the collateral) owes a duty to exercise all reasonable care
To obtain at least the market value of the collateral if it has a market value at the time of disposal
To obtain the best price reasonably obtainable at the time of disposal if the collateral does not have a market value

sections.source_quote: A secured party who disposes of collateral under section 128 (other than by purchasing the collateral) owes a duty, to any other person with a security interest in the collateral, and to the grantor, immediately before the disposal, to exercise all reasonable care: (a) if the collateral has a market value at the time of disposal—to obtain at least that market value; or (b) otherwise—to obtain the best price that is reasonably obtainable at the time of disposal, having regard to the circumstances existing at that time.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Secured party's statement of account obligations after collateral disposal
sections.summary: A secured party must provide a written statement of account to any other person with a security interest in the collateral or the grantor within 20 business days after request. The statement must detail the amount received, expenses, amounts paid to other secured parties, and the balance owed. This applies to disposals under section 128, including purchases, and must be provided before the end of the 20 business day period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires detailed accounting for collateral transactions, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments involving financial data. The obligation to provide statements affects how data is tracked and shared across borders.

requirements.text: A secured party must provide a written statement of account to any other person with a security interest in the collateral or the grantor within 20 business days after request
The statement must show the total amount received from disposal of collateral during the period starting when the collateral was seized and ending at disposal
The statement must show the amount of expenses relating to disposal
The statement must show any amounts paid to other secured parties
The statement must show the balance owing by the secured party to the grantor or by the debtor to the secured party

sections.source_quote: A secured party must, on request by any other person with a security interest in the collateral, or the grantor, give the person (or grantor) a written statement of account, if the first-mentioned secured party disposes of collateral under section 128 (including by purchasing the collateral in accordance with section 129).

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security agreement reinstatement and enforcement notices
sections.summary: A person may reinstate a security agreement by paying arrears and enforcement expenses, or remedying defaults. The grantor's right to redeem collateral has priority over other rights. A security agreement may be reinstated only once during its term. Secured parties are exempt from giving notices under specific conditions like failed attempts to locate persons or written waivers.
sections.dtia_summary: Reinstatement rules and enforcement notice exemptions impact cross-border security interests by defining when collateral can be reclaimed and when notices are unnecessary, affecting data transfer safeguards for international security agreements.

requirements.text: A person may reinstate the security agreement by paying arrears and enforcement expenses
A person may reinstate the security agreement by remedying any other default
The grantor's right to redeem collateral has priority over any other person's right
A security agreement may be reinstated only once during the period in which the security agreement is in force
A secured party is not required to give a notice if they failed to locate the person after reasonable attempts
A secured party is not required to give a notice if the grantor waives the right to receive the notice in writing
A secured party is not required to give a notice if a person other than the grantor waives the right to receive the notice in writing
A secured party is not required to give a notice if a court is satisfied that a notice is not required for any other reason

sections.source_quote: A person may reinstate the security agreement by: (a) paying arrears and enforcement expenses; (b) remedying any other default as a result of which the secured party proposes to dispose of, or retain, the collateral.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Registration of security interests across borders
sections.summary: Financing statements for security interests can be registered regardless of whether personal property is located in Australia or persons owning the property are located in Australia. Registrations must include specific data about secured parties, grantors, collateral descriptions, and end times. The registration process requires the applicant to believe on reasonable grounds that the secured party will take a security interest in the collateral.
sections.dtia_summary: Cross-border registration rules for security interests affect data transfers by allowing international collateral descriptions and requiring specific data elements, impacting how financial data is secured across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: A financing statement may be registered whether personal property is located in Australia or persons owning the property are located in Australia
The collateral must be described as consumer property or commercial property
The collateral must belong to a single class of collateral prescribed by regulations
The applicant must believe on reasonable grounds that the secured party will take a security interest in the collateral
The applicant must apply to amend the registration to end its effect with respect to the collateral if the secured party has never been a secured party in relation to the collateral

sections.source_quote: A financing statement, or a financing change statement, may be registered whether or not the personal property to which the statement relates is located in Australia or any person who owns or has rights in that property is located in Australia.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Verification statements for security registrations
sections.summary: The Registrar must provide verification statements to secured parties before and after registration events. If a registration event changes an address, the statement must be given to the previous and new addresses. If a secured party is omitted, the statement must be given to their previous address. Statement holders must notify grantors of the verification statement within reasonable time, with exceptions for commercial property and written waivers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires verification statements to be provided to secured parties and grantors, impacting data transfer assessments for security interest registrations across borders.

requirements.text: Registrar must provide verification statement to secured parties registered before and after registration event
If address changes, verification statement must be given to previous and new addresses
If secured party is omitted, verification statement must be given to previous address
Statement holder must notify grantors of verification statement within reasonable time
Verification statement may exclude specified data but must include other data
Statement holder may waive right to receive notice for commercial property

sections.source_quote: The Registrar must ensure that a verification statement in relation to a registration event is given to the following persons: (a) a person registered as a secured party in the registration immediately before the time of the registration event; (b) a person registered as a secured party in the registration immediately after the time of the registration event.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Registration effectiveness and defects
sections.summary: A registration is effective from registration time until the earliest of end time, amendment time, or when registration stops being available for search. Registrations become ineffective due to defects like seriously misleading data or specific defects outlined in section 165. Temporary effectiveness periods apply for certain defects, and secured parties must correct defects within 5 business days to maintain effectiveness.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines registration effectiveness and defect scenarios, impacting data transfer assessments for security interest registrations across borders.

requirements.text: Registration effective from registration time until earliest of end time, amendment time, or when registration stops being available for search
Registration ineffective due to seriously misleading data or specific defects in section 165
Defects may be temporary for up to 60 months or 5 business days after discovery
Secured party must amend registration within 5 business days to correct defects

sections.source_quote: A registration is effective from the registration time until the earliest of: (a) the registered end time; (b) an amendment time; (c) the time when the registration stops being available for search in the register.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Authorized data access for financial and legal purposes
sections.summary: This section defines permissible purposes for accessing the Australian Register of Financial Interests. It specifies that searchers like the Registrar, official receivers, legal representatives, and government entities may access data for purposes such as administering the Act, enforcing liens, bankruptcy proceedings, or maintaining legal order. The section prohibits unauthorized access or use of data beyond the specified purposes, with civil penalties for contraventions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for lawful data access that must be considered in cross-border data transfers involving financial records, ensuring only authorized entities can access data for specific legal purposes.

requirements.text: Searchers must not access the register otherwise than for the purpose specified in the item
Searchers must not use data obtained from accessing the register unless lawfully obtained from another source
The Registrar may investigate suspected contraventions of section 172(3)
Compliance with section 172(3) is taken as an obligation to the grantor

sections.source_quote: A searcher mentioned in an item in the table in subsection (2) must not, otherwise than for the purpose specified in the item: (a) access the register to search for data; or (b) use data obtained as a result of accessing the register, unless the searcher has also obtained the data lawfully from another source.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Conditions for third-party data access in financial registries
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions that must be met when accessing third-party data through the Australian Register of Financial Interests. It specifies that third-party data (e.g., motor vehicle information) may be included in search results and verification statements, but only if the applicant complies with conditions related to the use of such data. The section ensures that third-party data is not considered personal information under the Privacy Act and establishes obligations for both applicants and principals.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines conditions for accessing third-party data in financial registries, which are critical for DTIAs involving cross-border transfers of data that includes non-registered information.

requirements.text: The Registrar may arrange with third parties to provide access to non-registered data through the register
Third-party data does not include personal information under the Privacy Act 1988
Applicants must comply with third-party data conditions when applying for registration or access
Compliance with third-party data conditions is taken as an obligation to the third party

sections.source_quote: The Registrar may arrange with a third party (prescribed by the regulations) under this Part for the provision of access to non-registered data, held by the third party, through the register (as a portal).

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Amendment notices and registration changes for secured parties
sections.summary: This section outlines the administrative process for amendment notices and registration changes when a secured party requests modifications to a registration. The Registrar may issue amendment notices if they suspect the amendment is authorized under section 178. The amendment notice must be given within 5 business days after receiving a statement from the person who made the amendment demand. The Registrar must register a financing change statement amending the registration if the amendment notice is not responded to within the specified period, unless the Registrar suspects the amendment is unauthorized. The process includes judicial review options for disputes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer impact assessments by detailing the administrative procedures for modifying registrations, including the requirement for amendment notices and the Registrar's role in registering changes. It specifies timeframes and conditions for registration amendments, which could impact cross-border data transfers involving secured parties.

requirements.text: The Registrar may give the secured party an amendment notice if the Registrar suspects on reasonable grounds that the amendment demanded is authorized under section 178
An amendment notice must be given in response to a statement under subsection (3) as soon as practicable after the statement is given
An amendment notice must state the amendment demanded and invite the secured party to submit a response in writing within 5 business days
An amendment notice must set out the effect of section 181 (amendment of registration)
An amendment notice must include a copy of the statement if a statement is given under subsection (3)

If an amendment notice is given to a secured party after the end of the period covered by subsection (3), the Registrar must register a financing change statement amending the registration unless the Registrar suspects the amendment is unauthorized

The Registrar may register a financing change statement before the end of the period covered by subsection (3) if the secured party has responded and the Registrar has no reason to believe the secured party intends to give a further response
The period for the secured party to respond to an amendment notice is 5 business days unless extended by the Registrar
Data removed from the register because of an amendment must not be made available for search in the register by reference to any time before or after the time of removal

sections.source_quote: Section 180(1): The Registrar may give the secured party a notice (an amendment notice) of the amendment demanded.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Fees, administrative review and annual reporting for registration
sections.summary: This section establishes the fee structure for registration and searching the register, the administrative review process for registration decisions, and the obligation for the Registrar to prepare annual reports on the operation of the Act. The Minister may determine fees through legislative instruments, and the Registrar may approve payment arrangements for these fees. The Registrar must prepare annual reports detailing access refusals and fee compliance to demonstrate that fees do not constitute taxation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it governs the administrative processes and fees associated with registration, which could impact the cost and feasibility of cross-border data transfers involving secured parties.

requirements.text: The Minister may determine fees for registration and searching the register through legislative instruments
The Registrar may approve payment arrangements for fees by application in the approved form accompanied by the fee
Fees determined under this section must not amount to taxation
The amount of a fee is a debt due to the Commonwealth and may be recovered by application to court
The Registrar must prepare annual reports on the operation of the Act for presentation to Parliament
The annual report must include details of each occasion access to the register was refused or the operation suspended
The annual report must include information demonstrating fees do not constitute taxation

sections.source_quote: Section 190(1): The Minister may, by legislative instrument, determine fees for registration and searching the register.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Deputy Registrar resignation and termination procedures
sections.summary: This section outlines the resignation process for Deputy Registrars, requiring written resignation to the Minister with effect from receipt or a specified later date. It also details termination of appointments by the Minister through written instrument, with termination occurring if the Deputy Registrar ceases employment under the Public Service Act 1999.

requirements.text: A Deputy Registrar may resign by writing signed by him or her and given to the Minister
The resignation takes effect on the day it is received by the Minister or, if a later day is specified in the resignation, on that later day
The Minister may terminate the appointment of a Deputy Registrar by written instrument
The appointment of a Deputy Registrar is terminated if the Deputy Registrar stops being engaged under the Public Service Act 1999 for any reason

sections.source_quote: Section 202(1) and (2): A Deputy Registrar may resign by writing signed by him or her and given to the Minister. The resignation takes effect on the day it is received by the Minister or, if a later day is specified in the resignation, on that later day. Section 202(3): The Minister may terminate the appointment of a Deputy Registrar by written instrument. Section 202(4): The appointment of a Deputy Registrar is terminated if the Deputy Registrar stops being engaged under the Public Service Act 1999 for any reason.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Court jurisdiction and transfer procedures for PPS matters
sections.summary: This section establishes jurisdictional rules for courts handling Personal Property Security (PPS) matters, including which courts have authority, limits on jurisdiction, and procedures for transferring cases between courts. It specifies that transfers must be made to courts with appropriate jurisdiction and that the transferring court may recommend transfers to superior courts.

requirements.text: Jurisdiction is conferred on the Federal Court, the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1), the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), and courts of States and Territories for PPS matters
The Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) cannot award amounts exceeding $750,000 or a prescribed amount
Transfers between courts must follow specific criteria including the principal location of parties, place of events, and desirability of related proceedings in the same State or Territory
The transferring court may transfer the matter to the relevant superior court and recommend that the superior court transfer it to another court
The Registrar may intervene in judicial proceedings related to PPS matters

sections.source_quote: Section 205: Jurisdiction is conferred on the Federal Court, the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1), the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), and courts of States and Territories. Section 212: The transferring court may make a transfer under section 211 only if it appears to the transferring court that the transfer matter arises out of, or is related to, another proceeding pending before a receiving court or it is otherwise in the interests of justice that the transfer matter be determined by a receiving court.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security interest governing law determination
sections.summary: This section establishes rules for determining which jurisdiction's law governs security interests in various property types. It specifies that for goods, the law of the jurisdiction where the goods are located at attachment time governs validity and perfection. For intangible property, the law of the grantor's location governs. It includes exceptions for ADI accounts, intellectual property, and financial property, with specific rules for negotiable instruments and proceeds. The rules differ based on the type of property and whether the security interest is express or implied.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines how jurisdictional law applies to security interests across different property types, impacting cross-border data transfers involving financial assets by determining applicable legal frameworks for security interests.

requirements.text: The validity of a security interest in goods is governed by the law of the jurisdiction in which the goods are located when the security interest attaches
The perfection of a security interest in goods is governed by the law of the jurisdiction in which the goods are located at the time of perfection
The validity of a security interest in intangible property is governed by the law of the jurisdiction where the grantor is located when the security interest attaches
The perfection of a security interest in intangible property is governed by the law of the jurisdiction where the grantor is located at the time of perfection
A security interest in an ADI account is governed by the law of the jurisdiction that governs the ADI account
A security interest in a non-negotiable document of title is governed by the law of the jurisdiction where the goods are located when the security interest attaches
A security interest in a negotiable instrument not evidenced by a certificate is governed by the law of the jurisdiction that governs the negotiable instrument

sections.source_quote: Section 238: The validity of a security interest in goods is governed by the law of the jurisdiction (other than the law relating to conflict of laws) in which the goods are located when the security interest attaches, under that law, to the goods.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: State referral status and amendment references for personal property
sections.summary: This section defines when a State remains a referring State under the Personal Property Securities Act. A State continues as a referring State if its referral law is active, its amendment references have commenced, and the relevant version of the Act has not been terminated. It specifies that termination occurs when the referral reference ends, the amendment reference terminates or becomes restricted, or when the State's amendment reference for personal property (other than fixtures or water rights) terminates. The section also clarifies that termination of amendment references for fixtures or transferable water rights does not end the referring status.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which a State's referral status for personal property securities ends, impacting cross-border data transfers involving security interests. It defines when amendment references terminate and specifies exceptions for fixtures and water rights, which are relevant for data transfers involving property rights across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: A State is a referring State even if the State's referral law provides that the reference to the Commonwealth of a matter covered by subsection (3) or (4) terminates in particular circumstances
A State is a referring State even if the State's referral law provides that the adoption of the relevant version of this Act terminates in particular circumstances
A State is a referring State even if the State's referral law provides that any or all of the State's amendment references have not commenced in relation to a particular kind of personal property

A State is a referring State even if the reference to the Commonwealth Parliament of a matter covered by subsection (3) or (4) has effect only if the matter is not otherwise included in the legislative powers of the Commonwealth Parliament

A State is a referring State even if the reference to the Commonwealth Parliament of a matter covered by subsection (3) or (4) has effect only if the matter is included in the legislative powers of the Parliament of the State
A State stops being a referring State if the reference terminates
A State stops being a referring State if the adoption terminates
A State stops being a referring State if the State's amendment reference in relation to personal property (other than fixtures or water rights) terminates
A State stops being a referring State if the State's amendment reference in relation to personal property (other than fixtures or water rights) is qualified or restricted to any degree
A State does not stop being a referring State only because the State's amendment reference in relation to fixtures terminates
A State does not stop being a referring State only because the State's amendment reference in relation to transferable water rights terminates
A State does not stop being a referring State only because the State's amendment reference in relation to fixtures is qualified or restricted to any degree
A State does not stop being a referring State only because the State's amendment reference in relation to transferable water rights is qualified or restricted to any degree

sections.source_quote: Section 244(2) states: 'A State is a referring State even if the State's referral law provides that: (a) the reference to the Commonwealth of a matter covered by subsection (3) or (4) is to terminate in particular circumstances; or (b) the adoption of the relevant version of this Act is to terminate in particular circumstances; or (c) any or all of the State's amendment references have not commenced in relation to a particular kind (or kinds) of personal property (or so commence at or after the registration commencement time); or (d) the reference to the Commonwealth Parliament of a matter covered by subsection (3) or (4) has effect only: (i) if, and to the extent that, the matter is not otherwise included in the legislative powers of the Commonwealth Parliament (otherwise than by a reference under paragraph 51(xxxvii) of the Constitution); or (ii) if and to the extent that the matter is included in the legislative powers of the Parliament of the State.'

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security agreements: Effect of other laws on security interests
sections.summary: This section establishes that security agreements are subject to other laws where there is inconsistency between the Act and those laws. It specifies that certain Commonwealth Acts, including the Payment Systems and Netting Act 1998, prevail over this Act to the extent of inconsistency. The section also outlines restrictions on security agreements where state or territory laws apply, ensuring that security interests remain valid and enforceable despite conflicting laws.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by clarifying that security interests must comply with both federal and state/territory laws, requiring assessments of potential conflicts in registration, perfection, and enforceability across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: If there is inconsistency between this Act and one of the specified Acts, the other Act prevails to the extent of the inconsistency
Subsection 18(1) is subject to each of the following laws: Commonwealth law, State/Territory law, and general law
A law mentioned in subsection (2) does not apply to the extent affected by Division 4 (when this Act prevails)
A law mentioned in subsection (2) does not apply to the extent prescribed by regulations

sections.source_quote: If there is any inconsistency between this Act and one of the following Acts (the other Act), the other Act prevails to the extent of the inconsistency: (a) the Payment Systems and Netting Act 1998; (b) the Cheques Act 1986; (c) the Bills of Exchange Act 1909; (d) the International Interests in Mobile Equipment (Cape Town Convention) Act 2013.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Vesting of unperfected security interests in grantors during bankruptcy
sections.summary: This section details when unperfected security interests vest in the grantor upon events like bankruptcy, winding up, or restructuring. It specifies that security interests vest in the grantor immediately before certain events occur, such as the winding up of a company or the appointment of a bankruptcy administrator. The section also addresses exceptions where the security interest remains unaffected by these events.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by defining when security interests transfer to grantors during insolvency, requiring assessments of jurisdictional conflicts in security registration and perfection across different legal systems.

requirements.text: In the event of a company's bankruptcy or winding up, a secured party's unperfected security interest vests in the grantor
Security interests vest in the grantor immediately before the event that triggers winding up or bankruptcy
The security interest vests in the grantor when it attaches to the collateral if the grantor enters into a security agreement before the critical time
The security interest does not affect the title of a person who acquires property for new value without knowledge of the event

sections.source_quote: In the event of the bankruptcy of an individual grantor, or the winding up or the entry into administration of a body corporate grantor, a secured party's unperfected security interest vests in the grantor.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security interests unaffected by section 267
sections.summary: Section 268 specifies security interests that remain unaffected by section 267's vesting rules. These include security interests from transfers of accounts or chattel paper, commercial consignments, and security interests governed by foreign jurisdiction law. It also covers security interests under subsection (2) that involve subordinated debts between creditors.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies which security interests are exempt from the vesting rules in section 267, impacting cross-border security interest transfers and international debt structures.

requirements.text: Security interests provided for by transfers of accounts or chattel paper do not apply to section 267(2) and section 267A
Security interests in commercial consignments are exempt from section 267(2) and section 267A
Security interests governed by foreign jurisdiction law at the time mentioned in paragraph 267(1)(b) are exempt
Security interests covered by subsection (2) of this section are exempt

sections.source_quote: Section 268: (1) Subsection 267(2) and section 267A (security interests vested in grantor) do not apply to the following security interests: (a) a security interest provided for by any of the following transactions, if the interest does not secure the payment or performance of an obligation: (i) a transfer of an account or chattel paper; (iii) a commercial consignment; (aa) a security interest for which perfection, and the effect of perfection or non-perfection, is governed by the law of a foreign jurisdiction at the time mentioned in paragraph 267(1)(b); (b) a security interest covered by subsection (2) of this section.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Secured parties' information disclosure obligations
sections.summary: Section 275 mandates that secured parties disclose specific information to interested persons upon request, including security agreements, secured obligations, and property details. Requests must specify an address and be responded to within 20 business days. Secured parties may exempt responses if confidentiality agreements exist or if disclosure would contravene laws or duties of confidence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes disclosure requirements for secured parties, affecting data transfer impact assessments by defining when and how information about security interests must be shared across borders.

requirements.text: Secured parties must provide a copy of the security agreement upon request
Secured parties must provide a written statement of the amount or obligation secured by the interest
Secured parties must provide an itemised list of personal property attached to the security interest
Secured parties must respond to requests within 20 business days
Secured parties may exempt responses if confidentiality agreements exist
Secured parties may exempt responses if disclosure would contravene laws or duties of confidence

sections.source_quote: Section 275: (1) An interested person may request a secured party who holds a security interest in collateral to send or make available to the interested person, or any other person, any of the following: (a) a copy of the security agreement that provides for the security interest; (b) a statement in writing setting out the amount or the obligation that is secured by the security interest and the terms of payment or performance of the obligation, as at the day specified in the request; (c) a written approval or correction of an itemised list of personal property attached to the request indicating in which items of property the security interest is granted, as at the day specified in the request; (d) a written approval or correction of the following attached to the request, as at the day specified in the request: (i) the amount or the obligation that is secured by the security interest; (ii) the terms of payment or performance of the obligation.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Notices to secured parties: delivery methods and court orders
sections.summary: This section specifies how notices must be delivered to registered secured parties: by physical delivery, pre-paid post, fax, or email. It allows a single notice to cover multiple secured parties. It also permits courts to order alternative notice methods or dispensing with notices entirely, considering efficient administration and relevant factors.
sections.dtia_summary: Notices to secured parties must follow specific delivery methods but can be streamlined for multiple parties. Courts may override notice requirements under certain conditions, impacting cross-border data transfer compliance if notices involve international entities.

requirements.text: Notices must be delivered to secured parties by physical delivery, pre-paid post, fax, or email
A single notice may cover multiple secured parties
Courts may order alternative notice methods or dispense with notices entirely

sections.source_quote:
Part 8.5  Notices and timing
Section 288: a notice or document is required or permitted to be given to each of the secured parties. (2) The notice or document may be given to each of the secured parties by giving a single notice in accordance with section 287.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Onus of proof and constructive knowledge for secured parties
sections.summary: This section outlines who bears the burden of proof for certain facts in secured transactions, including security interest attachment, perfection by registration, and knowledge requirements. It defines constructive knowledge as knowledge an honest and prudent person would have under the circumstances, and specifies how knowledge applies to bodies corporate and related entities.
sections.dtia_summary: The onus of proof for security interests and knowledge requirements impacts data transfer compliance, particularly when determining whether data transfers constitute breaches of security agreements across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: The onus of proving security interest attachment, perfection, and knowledge lies with the person asserting those facts
Constructive knowledge means knowledge an honest and prudent person would have under the circumstances
For bodies corporate, knowledge is established if a director, employee, or agent had knowledge or if circumstances were communicated and would have been noticed with reasonable care

sections.source_quote:
Part 8.6—Onus of proof and knowledge
Section 295: This Part provides that the onus of proving certain facts lies with the person asserting those facts.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Initial application of security interests to personal property
sections.summary: This section specifies when the Australian Security Interests Act starts applying to new security agreements, security interests arising after commencement, transitional security agreements, interests in personal property, and migrated data from Commonwealth, State and Territory registers. It outlines specific provisions for the enforceability of transitional security interests, declared statutory interests, intellectual property licences, enforcement of security agreements, starting times for registrations, governing laws, and constitutional and non-constitutional interests. The Act applies at the registration commencement time (26 months after Royal Assent) for these matters.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the timeline and scope for when security interests in personal property become subject to the new Act, critical for determining when data transfers from existing registers to the Personal Property Securities Register occur and how transitional security interests are treated across borders.

requirements.text: This Act starts to apply to new security agreements at the registration commencement time
This Act starts to apply to security interests arising after commencement at the registration commencement time
This Act starts to apply to transitional security agreements at the registration commencement time
This Act starts to apply to new interests in personal property at the registration commencement time
This Act starts to apply to prescribed personal property at the registration commencement time
This Act starts to apply to migrated personal property data from Commonwealth, State and Territory registers at the registration commencement time
Transitional security interests are enforceable against third parties if they would have been enforceable under law before registration commencement
Section 73 (priority between security interests and declared statutory interests) applies to declared statutory interests
Section 106 applies to security interests in intellectual property licences made at or after registration commencement
Chapter 4 (enforcement of security interests) applies only to security interests provided for by security agreements made at or after registration commencement
A person may only apply to the Registrar for registration of financing statements at or after registration commencement time
The Registrar may only register financing statements at or after registration commencement time
Part 7.2 (Australian laws and those of other jurisdictions) applies only to security interests in collateral that arise at or after registration commencement time
Section 251 applies only to security interests in collateral that arise at or after registration commencement time
Section 252 applies only to security interests in collateral that arise at or after registration commencement time

sections.source_quote: Part 9.3—Initial application of this Act. This Act starts to apply to the following at the registration commencement time (26 months after the Act is given the Royal Assent, or another time determined by the Minister): (a) new security agreements; (b) security interests arising after commencement; (c) transitional security agreements and interests; (d) new interests in personal property; (e) prescribed personal property; (f) migrated personal property data from Commonwealth, State and Territory registers.

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Transitional security interest registration requirements
sections.summary: This section establishes rules for registering transitional security interests in personal property. Registrars must specify registration end times if applicable, disclose transitional security agreements, and ensure migrated security interests are perfected before registration commencement. The section defines when registrations are effective despite defects and specifies conditions for registration of transitional security interests.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific disclosures for transitional security interests to ensure proper cross-border data handling during migration periods. Defines when registrations remain effective despite defects, critical for assessing data transfer impacts during transitional periods.

requirements.text: State transitional registration end time as effective registration end time if subsection (4) applies
Disclose personal property is covered by transitional security agreement
Disclose transitional security agreement provides migrated security interest
Registration time is effective registration time for collateral
Transitional security interest perfected from immediately before registration commencement time
Registration effective despite defects if Registrar determines it
Registration becomes ineffective after defect correction period

sections.source_quote: Section 334(1) states: 'If data is registered with respect to personal property in a financing statement under subsection 333(2) on the basis that the property is in a class determined under subsection 333(1), but the personal property is not, in fact, in the determined class, this Act applies as if the data is not, and never has been, included in the register.'

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Circulating asset definitions for security interests
sections.summary: This section defines what constitutes a circulating asset for security interests, specifying conditions under which personal property is considered a circulating asset. It outlines control requirements for inventory and accounts, and establishes rules for ADI accounts. The section clarifies how security interests are treated in relation to different types of property.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines key terms for security interests across borders, critical for assessing how data transfers are handled under transitional security agreements. Specifies conditions for when security interests are perfected.

requirements.text: Personal property is a circulating asset if secured party has authority for transfers free of security interest
Circulating asset not applicable if effective registration discloses secured party control
Circulating asset not applicable if security interest perfected by possession
Control of inventory requires written agreement on specific appropriation
Control of accounts requires written agreement on deposits to ADI account

sections.source_quote: Section 340(1) states: 'For the purposes of this Act, if a grantor grants a security interest in personal property to a secured party, the personal property is a circulating asset if: (a) the personal property is covered by subsection (5) (unless subsection (2) or (3) applies); or (b) in any other case—the secured party has given the grantor express or implied authority for any transfer of the personal property to be made, in the ordinary course of the grantor's business, free of the security interest.'
